topic: Lints - missing_docs and API Documentation Enforcement

turns.content: I'm trying to submit my first contribution to this library crate, but rustc is complaining about #![deny(missing_docs). I thought since my struct is private I could just ignore the lint entirely without adding any doc comments at all. Why isn't that working?

The error occurs because #![deny(missing_docs) applies to public items, not private ones unless you explicitly suppress it or use rustdoc --document-private-items on nightly. Think of it like a security guard at the front door who only checks guests entering; your private fields are inside the building and don't need that check until they become part of the public API. If you want to document everything regardless, you can enable rustdoc --document-private-items in your build script or use #[allow(missing_docs)] on specific items you're intentionally keeping undocumented for now.

topic: Lints - missing_docs and API Documentation Enforcement

turns.content: Hey, I'm looking into adding better documentation to our library crate. What's the best way to enforce `missing_docs` warnings for internal modules but only deny it on public APIs?

You can use `#![warn(missing_docs)]` at the crate level to get warnings everywhere, then override with `#![deny(missing_docs)]` in your `lib.rs`. Inside private modules or structs, you can silence it locally by adding `#[allow(missing_docs)]`.
Wait, but if I use `#![warn(missing_docs)]`, won't that still enforce on everything? What if some parts are internal?

Right, it will. To target just the public API, you'd want `#![deny(missing_docs)]` in your `lib.rs`. Then for private items like functions or structs inside modules, use `#[allow(missing_docs)]`. This way, only what's exposed is strictly documented.
Ah, I see. And if I want to enforce example code snippets in doc comments too? Is that something I can do with lints?
As of Rust nightly, you can use `#![feature(missing_doc_code_examples)]` and then write examples inside your docs. But remember, these are still experimental.
Okay, so what about generating documentation reports? Are there tools to check how much of my crate is actually documented?

Yes! You can use `rustdoc --document-private-items` in your CI pipeline. It will generate docs for everything and flag missing ones. Alternatively, some teams use external tools like `cargo doc` with the `--open` flag to review.

Got it. So combining all these: enforce public API docs strictly (`#![deny(missing_docs)]`), suppress internal docs where needed (`#[allow(missing_docs)]`), enable nightly doc examples, and generate coverage reports post-build?
Exactly! That way, you get strict enforcement on the exposed API while keeping flexibility internally. And with nightly features like `missing_doc_code_examples`, you can add example-driven docs too.
